title: Heidegger and Boredom in Stranger Than Paradise (1984)
subtitle: Sitting with boredom and the journey to authentic living

title: The Trial by Franz Kafka
content:
I started *The Trial* having consumed some of its inescapable influence through Bulgakov's *The Master and Margarita*, Gilliam's *Brazil*, and Scorsese's *After Hours* (all personal favorites), so I already had the general gist of a man navigating an absurd bureaucratic nightmare in mind as I read.

What surprised me is how outrageously funny the book is. It’s not overtly ludicrous like *The* *Master and Margarita* (there are no alcoholic pistol-toting cats), and Josef K. is more outwardly subdued than Griffin Dunne’s frantic Paul Hackett. Still, the way Kafka plays ridiculous moments so straight-faced brought me to tears on the BART at least once.

When K.’s lawyer embarks on a lengthy monologue on the inner workings of the trial, Kafka declines to give a snapshot of the speech, instead making the reader sit through nearly 10 pages of legal rambling. There was nothing funny about each successive page of jargon, but eventually it was so absurd I just had to laugh. By inciting tedium, Kafka formally elicits in his audience an understanding of the hilarity in K’s powerlessness.

Anyways, I wasn’t surprised to learn that Kafka was often unable to finish reading drafts to his friends because he would laugh too much. He truly was a man fully committed to the bit and I commend him for it.

title: From Russia with Love (1963)
content:
**My first post!** Trying this experiment out in a dual effort to:

1. learn Claude Code and Github for my internship, and
2. document my thoughts on books, movies, and music.

I'm very excited! Since I don't have anything prepared, I'll paste this Letterboxd review from the last movie I watched.

From Russia with Love has Tintin-esque qualities — it's a flamboyant and whimsical international romp filled with quirky gadgets and larger-than-life characters, from a chess-playing spymaster to Bond's affable ally Kerim Bey. At the same time, the colorful constructed sets and vivid film stock, which create visual clarity in a way not unlike Hergé's ligne claire, belie a real, sinister sense of danger rooted in the Cold War tensions of the time. Terence Young blends these tones together, flipping from beautiful location shooting into visceral claustrophobic fights, then back into campy one-liners ("she's had her kicks") in a way that never feels jarring.

title: Morning Lemons
content:
There is a specific kind of quiet that only happens before 7am, when the kitchen is cold and the light comes in flat and gray. I cut three lemons this morning for no reason other than the smell. The tartness woke me up faster than the coffee did.

Some days the sourest thing is the best thing.

title: Rooftop Rhubarb
content:
The rhubarb on the roof finally came in. It is aggressively pink and aggressively sour, which is exactly the kind of plant I respect.

I made a small batch of compote and ate it straight from the pot with a spoon while it was still too hot. Worth the burnt tongue.

title: Green Apple Afternoon
content:
A whole afternoon spent doing nothing but slicing green apples and reading on the floor by the window. The cat judged me. I judged the cat back.

Sometimes a tart apple and a long book is the entire plan, and the plan is good.

title: Tamarind Night
content:
Tamarind is the underrated king of sour. Sticky, dark, a little funky. I soaked a block of it tonight and the whole kitchen smelled like a market I once walked through and never found again.

Dinner ran late. No one minded.

title: Pickled Everything
content:
I went a little far this week. Pickled onions, pickled carrots, pickled a single lonely cucumber that didn't make it into anything else.

The fridge now sounds like a tiny brine orchestra every time I open the door. I have no regrets.

title: First Cherries
content:
The first sour cherries of the season showed up at the corner stand. The good kind, the ones that make your jaw clench for a second before you reach for another.

I bought far too many and ate them on the walk home, leaving a trail of stems behind me like a very small, very tart breadcrumb path.
